narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've shown promising academic potential that many law schools value. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 41.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—below the average of 5.53—emerging as the single most influential one, as it signaled to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers, strongly pushing the outlook toward potential bar failure. Interestingly, your stronger undergraduate GPA played a major counterbalancing role, being the top positive influence by steering the prediction toward bar passage more than any other factor. Your LSAT score followed closely as the next strongest favorable factor, reinforcing the model's view of your capability to succeed. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your overall academics are competitive, but that Year 3 decile stood out as a red flag against the grain of your other strengths, enough to outweigh them in this close call. While disheartening, this isn't a definitive judgment on your potential—models like this weigh patterns from thousands of past cases, and individual stories often defy them. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps aiming for a higher LSAT if feasible, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded elsewhere—reach out to admissions counselors for feedback or explore post-bac programs to strengthen your record before reapplying. Your full-time commitment and family support are assets that could shine brighter with a bit more polish on those performance metrics. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
